sn4.21:1.2: that on one occasion the Blessed One was staying among the Sakyans at Silāvatī.
sn4.21:1.3: And on that occasion a large number of monks were staying not far from the Blessed One: heedful, ardent, & resolute.
sn4.21:1.4: Then Māra the Evil One, assuming the appearance of a brahman—with a large coiled top-knot, clad in an antelope hide, aged, crooked like a roof support, wheezing, holding a staff of fig wood—went to the monks and, on arrival, said to them,
sn4.21:1.5: “You have gone forth while young, masters—black-haired, endowed with the blessings of youth in the first stage of life—without having played with sensuality.
sn4.21:1.7: Don’t drop what is visible here-&-now in pursuit of what’s subject to time.”
sn4.21:1.8: “Brahman, we’re not dropping what’s visible here-&-now in pursuit of what’s subject to time.
sn4.21:1.9: We’re dropping what’s subject to time in pursuit of what’s visible here-&-now.
sn4.21:1.10: For the Blessed One has said that sensuality is subject to time, of much stress, much despair, & greater drawbacks;
sn4.21:1.11: whereas this Dhamma is visible here-&-now, not subject to time, inviting all to come & see, pertinent, to be known by the observant for themselves.”
sn4.21:1.12: When this was said, Māra the Evil One—shaking his head, wagging his tongue, raising his eyebrows so that his forehead was wrinkled in three furrows—left, leaning on his stick.
sn4.21:2.1: So the monks went to the Blessed One and, on arrival, having bowed down to him, sat to one side. As they were sitting there they [told him what had happened].
sn4.21:3.2: That was Māra the Evil One, come to blind you.”
sn4.21:3.3: Then, on realizing the significance of that, the Blessed One on that occasion spoke these verses:
sn4.21:4.1: One who has seen the cause from which suffering comes:
sn4.21:4.2: How could that person incline to sensuality?
sn4.21:4.3: Having realized that acquisition is a tie in the world,
sn4.21:4.4: a person should train to subdue just that.
